hook: The next great stock might be sitting in your kitchen cabinet, and you could spot it before any analyst on Wall Street does. Peter Lynch spent thirteen years proving that ordinary people, not the professionals, notice the winners first.

p: Peter Lynch ran Fidelity's Magellan fund and averaged about twenty nine percent a year for thirteen years, growing it into the largest mutual fund in the world. Yet he spends most of this book arguing that you, an ordinary person with a job and a grocery list, have a built in edge over the pros. You live as a consumer, and you notice great companies in everyday life long before the analysts in their glass towers get around to it.

The famous story is L'eggs. Lynch's wife Carolyn came home thrilled about a pair of pantyhose sold in little plastic eggs right by the supermarket checkout, where women shopped far more often than at a department store. Lynch investigated, found the maker was a company called Hanes, and bought it. It became one of the great winners of his early career, and the tip came from the checkout line, not a research report.

He collected these observations everywhere. Dunkin' Donuts, a plain company selling coffee and donuts that he could understand and watch thriving. La Quinta motor inns. Taco Bell. Toys R Us, where his kids and their friends flocked. The nurse who sees which drug actually works, the manager who watches one product fly off the shelf, all of them get the news weeks or even years before it reaches Wall Street.
sections.pull: The best stock tips do not come from Wall Street. They come from the checkout line, the parking lot, and the products your family loves.

p: Here is where people who only half read Lynch go badly wrong. Investing in what you know is the start of the process, not the end. Loving a product is a lead, a reason to investigate, and never by itself a reason to buy. A great product does not tell you the stock is cheap, whether the company is buried in debt, or whether that product is the whole engine of a small firm or a rounding error inside a giant. You still have to do the homework.

Lynch also tears down the wall of intimidation Wall Street builds. The efficient market theory says all information is already in the price and nobody can beat the market. His thirteen years of careful homework on ignored companies are his answer. Stocks are frequently mispriced, and fear, fashion, and neglect leave wonderful companies sitting at silly prices for a patient person who does the work.

The professionals, meanwhile, are handicapped. There is the street lag, the slow drip of permission that means by the time an institution is finally allowed to buy an exciting small company, the stock has often already tripled. A manager must justify every holding to a committee, and nobody gets fired for owning a famous name that sinks, while you can get fired for owning an obscure one that dips. So they hug the crowd. You answer to no committee.
sections.pull: The observation is the lead. The research is the decision.

sections.h: You Only Need A Few Tenbaggers
p: A tenbagger is Lynch's word, borrowed from baseball, for a stock that rises tenfold. Put in a thousand dollars, get back ten thousand. The liberating part of his argument is that you do not need many of them. A few in a lifetime will do.

In a basket of a dozen small stocks you can be wrong on most of them and still do wonderfully, because one or two enormous winners carry the whole portfolio. The most you can lose on any one is what you put in, but the winners have no ceiling. A single tenbagger can pay for a fistful of losers and then some.

This is why Lynch is not afraid of being wrong often. He is playing a game where losers fall to zero at worst and winners can run to the sky. You do not have to be right all the time. You have to be right about the big ones, and then you have to let them run.

Before you judge a company, Lynch says, know what kind it is, because you hold each type to a different standard. Slow growers are large, aging firms that expand only a little faster than the economy and pay a generous, dependable dividend, so you buy them for that reliable payout, not for a tenbagger. Stalwarts are the big, still growing giants like Coca Cola and Procter and Gamble, growing ten to twelve percent a year. You buy them cheap, ride them for a thirty to fifty percent gain, and they defend your portfolio in a recession because people keep buying soap and soda.

Fast growers are his favorites and where the tenbaggers live, small aggressive companies compounding twenty to twenty five percent a year. The key insight is that a fast grower does not need a fast growing industry. He often preferred a great company inside a boring, no growth industry, like a restaurant or retailer rolling out a proven formula, because little competition rushes in. The essential question is always whether it can keep growing and whether the runway is still long.

Cyclicals rise and fall with the economy, think autos, airlines, and steel, and they are the most misunderstood, because a cyclical looks cheapest, with the lowest price to earnings ratio, at the dangerous top of its cycle. Turnarounds are battered survivors coming back to life. Lynch's Chrysler was near collapse until he saw its cash, its coming minivan, and its capable leadership, and it became one of Magellan's biggest winners. Asset plays quietly sit on something valuable the market ignores, real estate, cash, a brand, or reserves worth more than the whole company's price. Categorize first, then judge by the right standard.

Before you buy, Lynch says, you should be able to give a two minute speech explaining why you own the stock. What the company does, why it is attractive, what has to happen for it to succeed, and what could go wrong. If you cannot tell that story in plain words a child could follow, you do not really understand what you own and have no business owning it. The drill is your protection against buying on a tip or a thrill.

He also paints the perfect stock, and the joke is that almost every trait would make an ordinary investor wince. He loves a company with a dull or ridiculous name, doing something dull or even disagreeable like handling waste, because that scares the crowd away and keeps the price low. He loves a spinoff, a company no institutions own and no analysts follow, one with a niche or a stranglehold, one selling things people buy over and over like razor blades and drugs and soda, and a user of technology rather than a maker of it. Above all he loves seeing insiders buy shares with their own money, because there is only one reason they buy, and a company buying back its own stock, which shrinks the share count and lifts every remaining owner's slice of the earnings.

The list of what to avoid is just as useful, because it is exactly what beginners chase. Be wary of the hottest stock in the hottest industry, whose price already assumes a perfect future. Be wary of any company sold to you as the next famous winner. Watch for diworseification, his word for a cash rich company making foolish acquisitions in businesses it does not understand. Avoid the whisper stock with a miracle story and no earnings, and the company that leans on a single customer who can squeeze it or walk away.

Everyday observation is only the lead, and the research is how you win. Lynch insists on a handful of numbers, and the most important is the price to earnings ratio, the price divided by earnings per share. The habit that matters is to never read it alone but always to compare it to the growth rate. His rule of thumb is that a fairly priced company's P E should roughly equal its growth rate. A company growing twenty percent a year is reasonable at a P E of twenty, a bargain at ten, and expensive at forty.

This is the seed of what later investors named the PEG ratio, the price to earnings ratio divided by the growth rate. Well below one may be a bargain, a company growing much faster than its price suggests. Well above one means you are probably overpaying. That single comparison, price against growth, will save you from countless expensive mistakes.

A few more checks. Look hard at the debt to equity ratio, because a company with little debt has staying power and can survive a mistake, while one buried in debt is fragile, and Chrysler's cash on hand was a big part of why Lynch knew it would survive. Prefer companies few institutions own and no analysts follow, so you arrive early. Watch inventories, because stock piling up faster than sales is a warning that products are not selling. And distrust a low price against book value, because the assets may be worth far less than the accountants claim.

sections.h: Water The Flowers, Cut The Weeds
p: Lynch's rule for selling is beautifully simple. You sell when the story has fundamentally changed, not when the price wiggles or the news sounds worried. You sell a fast grower when its growth clearly slows and the runway runs out, a stalwart after your thirty to fifty percent gain, a cyclical when the cycle turns down and inventories build, a turnaround once it is healthy again, and an asset play once the market finally recognizes the hidden value. In every case the trigger is a real change in the business, never a scary headline.

Then comes his most quoted warning. Too many investors spend their time watering the weeds and pulling the flowers. The flowers are the winners whose stories are still unfolding. The weeds are the losers whose stories have soured. Our nervous, loss averse minds do exactly the wrong thing, rushing to sell winners to lock in a profit and clinging to losers because we cannot admit a mistake. Do the opposite. Cut the weeds and let the flowers grow, because the winners you keep are the ones that become tenbaggers.

The last discipline is ignoring the noise. Tune out the forecasts about interest rates and recessions that nobody can reliably predict. Lynch joked that if you spend more than a few minutes a year worrying about the economy, you have wasted a lot of time. His cocktail party theory says that when everyone at the party, even the dentist, is eagerly telling you which stocks to buy, the market has likely reached a top. What matters is whether your companies keep growing their earnings, because the market follows the earnings in the end.
sections.pull: Cut the weeds and let the flowers grow. Most investors do exactly the opposite.

chart.title: The Growth Rate Spectrum
chart.subtitle: Typical yearly earnings growth for Lynch's three growth defined types
chart.caption: Illustrative. Cyclicals, turnarounds, and asset plays are judged by other measures, not by a steady growth rate.

takeaways: Start a watchlist from real life. Write down the companies behind the products and stores that you and the people around you genuinely love and use.
Pick the one company you understand best and run a two minute drill on it. What it does, which of the six categories it is, why it could win, and what has to keep happening.
Compare that company's price to earnings ratio against its earnings growth rate. At or below the growth rate is encouraging, far above it is a warning.
Glance at the balance sheet for one thing, the debt, to check whether the company has the staying power to survive a rough patch.
When a scary market headline or a confident economic forecast reaches you, practice letting it pass without acting on it.
Walk through any stocks you already own and ask of each, has the story changed or only the price. Cut the true weeds and let the real flowers grow.
